1.label: Question 1
1.scenario:
In its product range, Company A has an individual Critical Illness (CI) product with reviewable premiums. The terms and conditions of this product allow Company A to alter the premiums if the actual experience of morbidity or any other assumptions in the premium basis for the CI portfolio is different from what was originally expected.
Company A performs the premium reviews every 5 years.

i.question: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of offering reviewable premiums on its CI product from the perspective of Company A.

Advantages: Reviewable premiums provide the insurer with the mechanism to remediate future risks should actual experience turns out to be worse than pricing assumptions
For example, morbidity, mortality, expense, lapse experience
Lower margin for prudence required in premiums
Lower level of guarantee, which could lead to:

Reviewable premiums also provide the insurer the mechanism to pass the benefits of favourable experience back to the policyholders should actual experience turns out to be better than pricing assumptions, which could lead to:
Offering reviewable premiums may be more aligned to market / industry practice
Reviewability also gives Company A the opportunity to align the premium to changes in its own practices, e.g. its claims control procedures
It may allow Company A to offer cover for new risks at reasonable price even if it does not yet fully understand future claim experience for the risk
The availability of reinsurance for guaranteed CI products may be limited
The reinsurance terms offered by reinsurers on reviewable premium business could be better than those on guaranteed premium business
Company A may not have sufficient credible data for pricing and offering guaranteed premiums could be too risky against its risk appetite
Disadvantages: A CI product with reviewable premiums would create additional administrative burdens in comparison with a CI product with guaranteed premiums
Performing regular premium review is a complex process
Which may involve more frequent experience investigations
Any changes in premium rates will need to be updated in the IT systems
There will also be more frequent communications with the policyholders
This would add to the ongoing maintenance costs
Risk of mis-selling if sales intermediaries are not trained to explain the reviewability features adequately
Reputational loss / Damage to brand name
Frequent change in premium rates may not be liked by the policyholders
Increase in premium rates may not be well received by policyholders, which could lead to:
And if lapses are selective, it could worsen experience further
Policyholders may be particularly unhappy if their premium increases are as a result of policyholders who have claimed / lapsed (as neither of these things is their own fault)
Offering reviewable premiums may not be aligned to market / industry practice
It may also make the sales process more complex and increase the risk of mis-selling
Policyholder expectations restrict the extent to which the risks can be passed on
And risks are still retained in the 5-year periods between reviews
Costs of meeting additional regulations that may apply on reviewable business

ii.question:
Company A has an existing reinsurance arrangement with Reinsurer B. The reinsurance treaty for this arrangement is written on a risk excess of loss (XoL) basis that relates to the losses of each individual reviewable CI policy. Reinsurance premiums are reviewable and Reinsurer B performs the reinsurance premium reviews every 2 years.
Reinsurer B has decided to reduce its reinsurance premium rates in its most recent reinsurance premium review. In response to this, the Marketing Director of Company A has proposed reducing the premium rates currently being charged on its existing CI business with reviewable premiums.

Discuss the Marketing Director's proposal.

Arguments for the proposal: Reduced premium rates on its existing CI policies are likely to be well received by existing policyholders, which could lead to:
If lower premium rates are also reflected in new business, it will make the CI product more competitive in the market,
And higher sales could ultimately lead to higher profitability
The reduction may be of no cost to Company A if the reduction is closely aligned to the reduction in the reinsurance premium rates
If Company A retains some of the reduction in reinsurance rates, it would lead to higher profitability
The reinsurance rates may have been used in setting the original pricing assumptions, so it is fair to pass the benefit back to the policyholders
There could be processing efficiency in updating the change in reinsurance premiums and policyholder premiums in one single exercise
Which would make the change simpler to administer
The reinsurer is likely to have wider and more credible data for its analysis and more reliable results
Although this will be reliance on the reinsurer's wider experience being relevant to Company A's specific policyholder portfolio
As reinsurance premiums may be based on the reinsurer's aggregate experience (from all ceding insurers, not just Company A,
Where these other ceding insurers may offer CI product with different product features (e.g. different CIs / CI definitions)
If Company A reinsures a significant proportion of its reviewable CI policies to Reinsurer B, the risk of misalignment of their respective underlying experience would be proportionately reduced
Arguments against the proposal: If Company A does not review its premiums (i.e. not passing the benefits of reduced reinsurance premiums to its policyholders), it will increase its profit margin
The key risk is the degree of the misalignment of underlying experience between Company A and Reinsurer B
It is unclear which aspects of Reinsurer B's experience are reflected in the reduction of the reinsurance rates, which could be in respect of:
Company A should seek to understand which aspects of the above experience before making an appropriate decision
Morbidity: Given that the reinsurance arrangement is on an individual excess of loss basis, the experience between the insurer and the reinsurer could be very different if:
And the portfolio of Company A's business is predominantly of lower sum assured
The higher sum assured cases may be subject to more stringent underwriting process
And claims experience may be much better for reinsurer under such policies
Aggregations of many small claims which are below the excess point (even though the excess point is set at a reasonable level) will hit Company A but not necessarily the reinsurer
The experience for higher sum assured may therefore be significantly different from lower sum assured policies
The upper limit / ceiling of the reinsurance cover means that for some very large claims, the top slice of which revert to Company A, which will have a more proportionate impact on Company A
Expenses: The expense loading may be significantly higher for the insurance premium compared to reinsurance rates due to:
Reduction in insurance premium in similar proportion to reinsurance rates would assume that Company A and Reinsurer B have similar expense experience
Given that Company A and Reinsurer B are different organisations, they may have very different expense bases

Communications to individual policyholders could incur higher administrative expense to the insurer (Company A) as opposed to the reinsurer's (Reinsurer B) communication to its cedants / ceding insurers as the volume of policyholders is likely to be much larger than the volume of cedants
Lapse: The considerations are similar to those under morbidity
In particular that the reinsured cases exhibit different lapse experience from the overall CI portfolio within Company A
Changes in regulatory/tax requirements: These changes may be specific to reinsurers.
If that is the case, the benefits should not be allowed for the non-reinsured portion of the business
Other considerations: There is a misalignment between the review period - a 5-year cycle for Company A and a 2-year cycle for Reinsurer B
The change in reinsurance premium rates may therefore be more frequent than Company A's own review exercise
Following the reinsurer's rate change means that Company A is placing more reliance on Reinsurer B's analysis within its own review cycle
Which may not be acceptable from Company A's own risk management framework/ policy
Frequent changes in premium rates may have negative effects on policyholders
In particular that any premium reductions are followed by subsequent premium Increases
This could lead to policyholder dissatisfaction / complaints
Which could adversely affect lapse experience
The practice could also attract press attention
And even intervention/investigation by the regulator
If the reduction is not material, it may not have the desired level positive impact on policyholder satisfaction
Which could be outweighed by the implementation costs
There will also be the extra costs of this additional review to factor into premiums

2.label: Question 2
2.scenario:
Company A is a large health and care insurance company, and a market leader in both Income Protection (IP) and Private Medical Insurance (PMI) products in Country X.
The IP policies are written on a guaranteed premium basis while the PMI policies are written on an annually renewable basis.
Country X has recently implemented a new regulatory solvency regime that resulted in a significant increase in the solvency capital requirements relating to Company A's IP business.

i.question: Suggest possible reasons why the new regulatory solvency regime could impact the solvency capital requirements for Company A's IP business more than its PMI business.

Indicative Solution: The main reason is that the IP product has a guaranteed premium throughout the policy term whilst the PMI product's premium is only guaranteed for one year,
Which may be impacted differently due to the impact of different calculation methods
And/or requirements over the assumptions that are used for the two product lines
Insurance risk capital requirement: The longer period of guarantee means higher reserve
For which margins for uncertainty in cashflows would be higher the further into the future the cashflows are (impacting long-term business more than PMI)
Higher reserve could lead to higher capital requirement
If capital requirement calculation is linked to level of reserve
Or there might be an explicit capital requirement for guarantees
The factors used for calculating the capital requirement could be higher for products with higher guarantees
The volume of IP business could be significantly higher than the volume of PMI business
The IP business could have much lower level of reinsurance
Or limited mitigating effects can be allowed for in the capital requirement calculation
Catastrophe risk capital requirement could be high on IP product
Lapse capital requirement could be higher as longer term product are exposed to higher lapse risk
In particular that initial / upfront costs /expenses are recouped over the entire policy Term
Maintenance expenses are likely to be higher on IP products
And are exposed to higher risk of future inflation
Investment risk capital requirement: Higher reserve on IP products means higher level of assets is need to back liabilities
Due to the long term nature of liabilities, the assets are likely to be long-term assets
In contrast to the assets backing PMI, which are likely to be predominantly in cash / short-term deposits
Lower liquidity relating to the IP asset portfolio could mean higher liquidity capital requirement
IP asset portfolio may consist of equity or property asset types, which could be considered as assets with high level of volatility
Higher level of volatility relating to market value and future return would lead to higher capital requirement
Fixed interest assets may be used to back the IP claims in payment reserves
Which is likely to have a higher capital charge than cash assets
Counterparty/Default capital requirement: If the IP portfolio is heavily reinsured, then the exposure to reinsurance default would be higher than PMI
If the administration services are outsourced to third party, the exposure to third party default would be higher than PMI
The level of premiums collected and held by intermediaries could also be higher for IP products
Both in terms of amount and/or holding period
Other: Different operational aspects of the IP and PMI business could lead to different operational risk capital requirement
Regulator may have imposed a capital add-on on Company A's IP business
Solvency capital requirements for Company A's PMI business may have been calculated using an internal model, while the standard formula may have been used for the IP business

ii.question:
Company A's Finance Director has proposed the following options to reduce the financial impact on the company's higher solvency capital requirements:
(a) reinsure a significant proportion of the IP portfolio to reinsurer(s) that are external to Company A.
(b) stop writing new IP business and run off the existing IP portfolio over time.
(c) sell the entire IP portfolio to another insurer.

Discuss the advantages and disadvantages for Company A of each of the Finance Director's proposed options.

Reinsurance of IP business: Advantages:
The solution could be put in place quickly
The effect would be immediate as soon as the reinsurance arrangement is in place
Reinsurance is effective at passing on insurance risk
Depending on the reinsurance terms, it could have all the usual advantages under reinsurance, which could include:
Improved solvency position, lower new business strain, financial assistance, technical expertise, increasing capacity to accept risk (volume), ability to write larger risks (individual size)
Depending on the local tax / solvency capital requirements, the reinsurer may be able to pass the tax / solvency capital requirement arbitrage benefits to Company A
Company A retains the relationship with its customers (e.g. for future cross-selling opportunities)
Company A can carry on writing new business
Reinsurance benefits may also lead to more competitive premium rates
Which could lead to more new business / market share
Disadvantages:
Increased dependency on reinsurer
And hence reinsurer default exposure
Although this could be managed / diversified through using multiple reinsurers
Reinsurance may cost more (high cost of reinsurance cover)
Or become more expensive
If the new regime also affects reinsurance business
May not be sufficiently effective if reinsurance level is already high
It would increase reinsurance default capital requirement
Suitable reinsurance may not be readily available in the market
Or not available at an acceptable price
Additional administrative / maintenance expenses relating to the reinsurance arrangements
Stop writing New Business: Advantages:
Solvency capital would not increase further
Provided that other risk profile of the business does not change
Overall business volume shall reduce over time
IP business could be loss making
Company A could just focus on its PMI business
This could enhance the company's brand name / reputation in the PMI market
This action should be quick and easy, as stop writing new business does not require much effort in the very short term
Disadvantages:
Higher solvency capital requirements do not necessarily mean that the IP business is now loss making / unprofitable
Loss of potentially profitable future business could have negative impact on financial position of company over longer term
Reduced market share
Stop selling new business may not be taken positively by the market
If IP business is profitable, Company A will lose potential profits of future new business
Overhead expenses per policy may be increased for the remaining business
Potential issues relating to re-allocation of staff engaged in IP business
Although initially it will just be those who are involved in pricing / underwriting / new business sales
Expenses may rise in the short term as a result of staff redundancies (e.g. of new business processing, underwriting departments etc)
The effect will not be instantaneous
The benefit will therefore be limited if Company A's solvency position requires immediate remedial actions
Distribution channels / Intermediaries may not consider it positively due to reduced product categories
There is a risk of an increase in lapses once this is communicated
And potentially affecting the valuation, particularly as these are likely to be selective
Additional expense reserve requirements may be required for a closed block of business
Any risk diversification benefits between the two products would be reducing over time as the IP business is running off
Sell entire IP portfolio: Advantages:
Effect will be instantaneous as soon as the business is sold
Better focus on PMI business
Which could lead to improved services to short term business
All the reserves and capital requirements will be released
Immediate realisation of profits from IP business
Which could be used for expanding PMI business
All the ongoing costs relating to maintaining the IP business would eventually be eliminated
Disadvantages:
Selling existing business may not be taken positively by the market
Overhead expenses per policy may not be eliminated in the short term
Economies of scale could be lost
There will be more staff to be re-allocated than the other two options
Which could cause concerns / insecurity amongst staff within the company
There may not be a suitable buyer in the market
Or the purchase price offered is not financially attractive
In particular if it is much lower than the embedded value of the IP business
Cost of removing/ re-allocating the excess staff could be high
Distribution channels / Intermediaries may not consider it positively due to reduced product categories
The cost of a mergers and acquisitions activity could be substantial
Tax treatment of such deal should be considered
Customer might prefer buying both contracts from the same insurer
This could lead to lower renewal rates on PMI business
Rebalancing cost of matching assets due to change of nature of liabilities
Any risk diversification benefits between the two products would be lost
Company could become overly exposed to risks from PMI business

iii.question:
Company A has decided to sell its entire IP portfolio. Company B is a large health and care insurance company that specialises in selling IP products in Country X.
Company B is one of the insurers invited by Company A to make an offer on its IP portfolio.

Discuss the factors that Company B may consider in arriving at a purchase price for Company A's existing IP portfolio.

General / Commercial considerations: Quality and granularity of information disclosed in the tendering documents
Implications on marketability of Company B's brand image / reputation
Company A's reputation in the market
Any known regulatory issues relating to Company A's IP business
Size of Company A's block of business relative to Company B's overall portfolio
Compatibility of Company A's IP products
Risk of attracting regulator's attention relating to monopoly issues
Company B may also consider any diversification benefits in taking on the business, e.g. by occupation or geographical location etc
Or any potential accumulations of risk, e.g. by occupation or geographical location etc
Company B's negotiating strength may affect the price it is willing to offer
As will its keenness to win the business
Potential benefits of leveraging Company A's distribution channels / network
And cross-selling to Company A's existing customer base
Data considerations: Need to consider how reliable the available data is for determining the offer price
Individual policy data may not be provided by Company A
Which may be the case at the initial stage / round of the bidding process
Certain data fields needed may not available
Or data may not be available in the desired format
Company B may need to consider what adjustments would be needed
And factor any uncertainties in the offer prices
Or legal terms and conditions in the offer documents
Methodology considerations: The starting point for determining the value of a block of long-term business for an acquisition is likely to be the embedded value (EV) / present value of future profits (PVFP)
However an important additional element is likely to be the goodwill
The value of the goodwill corresponds to the potential of future business sales from this purchase
The offer price is therefore likely to be to be the sum of the embedded value and goodwill
Given that Company B is a large IP specialist, the goodwill from a block of in-force IP business from Company A is likely to be limited
Needs to consider the active/non-claims policies and claims in payment policies separately
For active policies, may consider using the inception / disabled life annuity approach
For claims in payment, consider the disabled life annuity only
For both types of policies, a projected cashflow approach could be used
It would involve projecting forwards futures cashflows and discounted back at the required risk discounted rate
The cashflow items for the non-claims policies would include premiums, claims costs, maintenance expense, capital costs and tax
Claims cost would be calculated as: inception rates x disabled life annuity.
The cashflow items for the claims in payment policies would include benefit payments, claims expenses, capital costs and tax
Allow appropriately for any existing reinsurance arrangements
Assumptions consideration: Morbidity (Inception rates):
The starting point would be based on Company A's experience
This may not be provided in sufficient level of detail given that this is a mergers and acquisitions situation
Company B may make use of its own experience to supplement the limitation in Company A's data
Needs to carefully consider any similarities and differences between the products in terms of:
Policy features / conditions
Underwriting procedures / standards
Claims management procedures / standards
Policyholder profile
Which may be different due to the use of different distribution channels
Termination rates:
Need to consider mortality and recovery rates separately
Consider experience data provided by Company A as a starting point
And supplement it using own data if necessary
Recovery rate
Lapses:
Needed for non-claims / active policies only
Consider any potential implications of the sales on future persistency
Expenses:
Expenses should be based on Company B's own expense assumptions
As the business will be transferred and managed by Company B post acquisition
Consider any potential expense efficiency that can be factored into the assumptions
Given that taking on a block of existing business may mean overhead expenses being spread over a larger volume of business
Take into consideration any existing third part arrangements that Company A had with its services providers
Allow appropriately for expense inflation
Again should be based on Company B's best estimate view
Allow for the acquisition costs
And subsequent costs of integration, e.g. systems changes, staff training, etc
There may be additional investment costs to factor in, e.g. disinvesting / reinvesting of assets
Capital costs:
Allow appropriately for cost of capital requirements
Which could be based on Company B's own internal risk appetite / policy
This will include the level of capital requirement
And the costs to be charged for holding this capital requirement
Allow appropriately for any potential diversification benefits from a larger and more diversified policy portfolio
Tax
Allow appropriately for tax based on Company B's own tax position
And the potential tax implications of the acquisition
Risk Discount Rate: This should be in line with the required return that Company B seeks to achieve from buying this block of business
Taking into consideration its own risk strategy
And riskiness / uncertainties underlying Company A's IP business

3.label: Question 3
3.scenario:
Company A is a health and care insurance company that specialises in accelerated and standalone individual Critical Illness (CI) products. With the aim of improving the marketability of its CI products, the Product Development Director has proposed adding the following features to new CI policies:
(a) extending the list of illnesses covered.
(b) a continuation option where the policyholder can choose, without having to provide evidence of health, to continue the cover provided by the policy under circumstances where it would have otherwise ceased.
(c) tiered benefits where for one or more of the illnesses covered, the payment of the sum insured is linked to the severity of the disease.

i.question: Describe the risks associated with each of the above features from the perspective of Company A.

General risks applicable to all 3 propos: Reinsurance risk as re-negotiation with reinsurer for the revised features would be required
There is a risk that reinsurance cover may no longer be available
Or available at a high cost
Additional product features could lead to an increase in reserves and solvency capital requirements
There is a risk of lapse and re-entry as these changes are for new business only
Expense risk as there are development costs involved
And additional training may be needed for the claims management staff
And upgrading IT system to accommodate new features
Regulatory risk as the new feature may require approval
Sales risk as the additional features may not attract sufficient new business
Which could lead to expense costs if additional new business is not sufficient to offset development costs
Especially if the competitors are not offering these features
Marketing risk - if the additional features do not actually meet customer needs
Extending the list of CIs specific risks: Increased claims cost if no additional premium is not being charged
If the insurer is intending to charge future policyholders for the additional illnesses, there could be pricing risks,
As the current morbidity assumptions may no longer be valid
And may not be available data to set the assumptions
Pricing risk as experience data will not include the additional illnesses
Reserving risk due to the higher level of uncertainty relating to a longer list of illnesses
The potentially higher number of claims may also increase claims handling expenses
If the CIs that are added are not well-known / feared by the public, then they might not be valued
Mis-selling risk if intermediaries were not properly trained to explain precisely what the additional illnesses are
Definitions of new illnesses covered may not be standardised
May lead to more claim disputes
And damage to brand name / reputational risk
Offering Continuation Option specific ri: Sub-standard lives are more likely to opt for this option
Especially if the premium rates have not been priced adequately

If the circumstances that lead to the termination of the original policy is anything other than reaching the end of the policy term (e.g. "after a claim has been made" or "following the non-payment of premiums"), then the selection risk may be even higher
Pricing risk due to the difficulties involved in option pricing
And lack of credible experience data
Pricing assumptions may no longer be valid e.g.
Mix of business may change as a result
Average age of policyholders under this portfolio may increase
Reserving risk due to the uncertain nature of options
Intermediaries may not like this feature if further commission is not payable when the policyholders exercising the option
Offering Tiered Benefits specific risks: Pricing risk as experience data may not be available
The new feature is likely to accelerate the timing of some of the claims
In some cases, it will increase claims cost as previously the claim may not be made unless the disease is of a certain severity
Mis-selling risk if the policyholders still expect full sum assured to be paid on the diagnosis of certain illnesses
And this could lead to complaints
And unwanted attention from the press/media
Sales risk as tiered benefits may not be popular with potential policyholders
As they may perceive this as a way for the insurer to pay out lower amounts
Rather than paying out the appropriate claim amounts which correspond to the severity of the conditions
And hence the corresponding medical and lifestyle supporting costs
Reserving risk due to the added complexity of timing of claims payment
Operational risk due to admin staff strain as a result of the multiple payments

If the severity of the early stages of illness has not been appropriately defined (e.g. if the claims definitions / conditions are misaligned with the actual stage of severity of the illness), a tiered benefits could increase the risk of windfall payments.

ii.question: Suggest possible actions Company A may take to mitigate the risks identified in part (i) above.

General risks mitigation: Appropriate training to sales staff to minimize mis-selling risk
Check the availability of reinsurance cover before making a decision on adding the new features
Obtain reinsurance assistance on:
Pricing
Underwriting
Product design
Claims management
Financial assistance / financial reinsurance to help finance the reserving / solvency capital requirements
Align reinsurance treaty terms with the revised features
Include different layers of reinsurance with different reinsurers to minimize the credit risk
Perform sufficient investigations in setting the appropriate pricing assumptions
Through seeking external help
Making appropriate use of additional data sources, e.g. industry / population data,
And incorporate adequate margins for uncertainty
Allow appropriately for any potential changes in:
Expense assumptions
Morbidity experience
Lapse assumptions
Mix / profile of business
Reserving and solvency capital requirements
Consult regulator in terms of product design approval
Manage sales risk through market research / survey
Offer reviewable premium rates to minimise guarantee risk.
Strict underwriting at proposal / claims stage
Introduce / increase any waiting periods
Manage marketability risk through appropriate marketing campaign
Ensure clear and unambiguous policy wordings to minimise mis-selling risk
To reduce lapse and re-entry risk, the features could also be offered to existing business
The ultimate risk mitigation would perhaps be not adding the new features if they are considered to be too risky
Extending the list of CIs specific risks: Ensure that the additional illnesses are consistent with market standard
Changes could be introduced in stages (a few illnesses at a time) to assess the impact
Offering Continuation Option specific ri: Set maximum level of sum assured for the continuation option
Offering Tiered Benefits specific risks: To reduce the complexity for tiered benefits, could restrict the number of tiers
Or introduce the changes in stages
And/or the number of illnesses that use tiers

4.label: Question 4
4.scenario:
The government of a small developing country (Country B) is considering increasing the state healthcare support to its citizens. The methods of state healthcare support considered by the government include:
(a) direct provision.
(b) lump sum cash payment.
(c) regular income.

i.question: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of each of the above methods of state healthcare support from the perspective of the government.

Direct Provision: The government is in direct control of the quality standards of the state healthcare facilities hence can ensure that population needs are met
The government will be able to control and manage expenditure and reduce wastage since the facilities will be run by government functions
The approach is in line with the paternalistic goal of the government
The government will be providing a guaranteed service which may turn out to be expensive / unaffordable for the government
And at the cost of spending less on other non-healthcare support services. e.g. public transport, social housing, social security / unemployment benefits etc
The government may struggle to maintain the standards if it fails to sustain the cost of running the expenses
Which may cause dissatisfaction in the society
And potentially losing support from its citizens
Due to government bureaucracy, there may be wastage and abuse of resources under the state healthcare facilities
The government may not have the necessary expertise to provide all the services required by its citizens
Lump Sum Cash Payment: Advantages:
The approach empowers the citizens to use the money at their own discretion
It provides the citizens with flexibilities and options
It may therefore better serve each citizen's unique healthcare needs
It may provide the citizens in need more confidence that they are being well looked after by the government
If the lump sum is a fixed amount for specific healthcare provisions (i.e. not on an indemnity basis), then there is certainty over the ultimate cost
Disadvantages:
Citizens may abuse the payment and use it for non-healthcare expenses leading to wastage and the approach not meeting its intended goal
Citizens may not to be sophisticated enough to make the best use of the availed money,
Or make appropriate financial planning
Especially for a long time period
There is a risk that the lump sum exceeds the healthcare costs, so the individuals having extra to do what they want with at the expense of the government
On the other hand, the lump sum payment may be significantly lower than the healthcare costs, then this may be unpopular with the general population / electorate
A decision needs to be made about the amount of the lump sum to pay in each case, which will be a big burden administratively.
The burden of paying out lump sums in a year where the population experience exceptionally adverse health issues could affect the government's fiscal position significantly
This may include increase in money supply in the economy which can lead to increase in inflation in the economy
Private healthcare providers may increase prices to take advantage of the lump sum cash payment
Maybe be viewed by the healthier lives as a windfall payment to those who make a claim
Regular Income: Advantages:

The burden of paying out regular income (where the overall costs are spread over future years) in a year where the population experience exceptionally adverse health issues would have less significant impact on the government's fiscal position
And hence will be a better way of cashflow management for the government
There will not be a sudden shock to the monetary and fiscal positions
And hence unintended consequences like adverse impact on inflation is unlikely to happen
There is reduced chance of citizens abusing the money and being left without provisions for the future
The regular income can be reviewed consistently to match trends in the market and ensure adequacy
Disadvantages:
Additional burden on the government to manage regular payments,
And ongoing assessment of the appropriate level of regular income
And validity of claiming ongoing payments
The regular payments may not be aligned to the actual pattern of medical costs
Receiving regular payments may encourage citizens to continue claiming even if their conditions have improved

ii.question: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of each of the above methods of state healthcare support from the perspective of the citizens of Country B.

Direct Funding: Citizens are guaranteed of services for free and not exposed to higher pricing from private health care providers
It does provide them with a safety net
The citizens do not need to worry about being financially literate to make the correct decisions about how to spend government payments
General facilities may be well distributed geographically across the country, providing easy access to citizens and hence no need to travel long distances to get access to the facilities
The government may not be able to meet all citizens healthcare requirements
Quality of service may be poor which could include:
The potentially low quality standard could add to the stress of an already ill citizen
And increase chances of medical complications
Some highly specialist procedures / surgery may not be readily available domestically
The domestically available specialist facilities may not be well distributed geographically across the country and some citizens may have to travel long distances to get access to the facilities
Citizens may have a lack of choice over where, when and who provides the treatment
Lump Sum Cash Payment: Advantages:
Citizens are likely to welcome the financial security that a lump sum payment would bring
And the flexibility on how to make use of the money
In the best way that meets their medical needs
Individuals have the possibility of a windfall with a lump sum
Disadvantages:
There is the risk that some citizens may make fraudulent claims
There is the risk that the citizens may make incorrect financial decisions
And squander the money on non-medical expenditure
Some may find it a burden, in particular those who are elderly or suffering from serious illness
The lump sum payment may not be sufficient for some citizens to cover all their medical costs
The lump sum cash payment may not be adequate to cater for the citizen's lifetime long-term care needs
The citizens are exposed to investment risks when they try to save the money for funding future long-term care use
There is still a possibility that the quality of service / length of waiting lists / access to drugs provided by private healthcare providers is poor
Regular Income: Advantages:
Easier for the citizens to budget and plan since the money is availed regularly
And of much smaller amount
There is reduced risk of making wrong financial decisions
Since there is reduced scope of misspending
Reduced investment risks since the regular income can be reviewed accordingly and change in line with medical conditions
Cashflow management at household level is easier compared to receiving a once off lump sum
The incentive for citizens to make fraudulent claims is lower than that under lump sum cash payment
Disadvantages:
There is still the risk that some citizens may make fraudulent claims (although the incentive to do so is lower than that under the lump sum option)
The regular income may not align to the medical costs of all the different medical conditions
For example a large lump sum payment may be needed to cater for major medical procedures
Some citizens may find it difficult to give up receiving the regular payments
And may risk making fraudulent claims
There is still a possibility that the quality of service / length of waiting lists / access to drugs provided by private healthcare providers is poor
There is a risk that individual citizens may need to meet some of the costs themselves due to timing issues relating to the regular income payments
A regular income also leaves individual citizens liable to make incorrect financial decisions with the money

iii.question:
The government of Country B currently uses the 'pay-as-you-go' method to fund its welfare expenditure. It has been proposed that a system of 'forward funding' could replace the 'pay-as-you-go' method.

Discuss the factors that the government of Country B may consider before replacing the 'pay-as-you-go' method with the 'forward funding' method.

Affordability / Financial Requirements: The government will need to consider whether it has adequate revenues to make the transition from "pay-as-you-go" to "forward funding"
There will be a huge financial requirement initially (as currently younger members of the population will be paying the benefits of older members)
The government will need to estimate the cost of this, including:
It will also need to consider the practicalities of how the funding is collected, e.g. directly through taxation
The government can consider whether a special tax should be introduced and its proceeds segregated for the forward funding model
Alternatively, the government can consider raising funding from partners such as multilateral financial institutions and United Nations organisations like the World Health Organisation
The government could consider issuing debts / government bonds to fund up front cost
Which would depend on how easy it is to raise money and the cost of borrowing
The government may need to consider implications of the proposed "forward funding" on the affordability of the level of state healthcare provision that it aims to provide
Investment of funds: The government will need to set up investment function to invest the funds until they are needed
Which will incur asset / investment management costs
The government will need to formulate a suitable investment strategy
Positive investment returns on assets built up could reduce overall cost of healthcare funding
But poor investment performance will add further burden to the government's funding commitments
Period of Funding: The government will need to consider the period they expect the forward funding model to be effective
This may be over a period of 5 years, 20 years or into perpetuity
The chosen period will be the target period over which their budget should balance
Significantly long periods may mean that projections require a margin for uncertainty
Population and Workforce trends: The population size and its growth rate will impact on the overall cost
The government needs to consider how the workforce and population are expected to change in the future
As a pay-as-you-go system will struggle with an ageing population
Due to the fact that the working population will not be able to keep up with the needs of those receiving benefits
Whereas a forward funding method will not have this issue
Expertise and admin systems: The government needs to consider the expertise that it will need to recruit in order to manage the fund efficiently.
Such expertise will include specialists such as: investment advisors, actuaries, accountants, legal experts, administrators, medical experts, demographics experts etc.
Admin systems will need to be updated so that they can support a forward funding model
Citizen's data may need to be checked / validated / cleansed before they are transferred to the new system
Development expenses would need to be considered; these could include:
Political and other considerations: The government needs to consider whether this proposal is in line with its political objectives
Which could be a promise it made in the last election
The government should also consider the models adopted by its neighbouring countries
In particular those that have similar cultural and social infrastructure
And the performance of the "forward funding" funding methods in those countries
The government needs to consider the underlying reasons for this proposal
And consider carefully what the other options are
Given the immediate need for immediate funding, the government would need to
Consider the implications on its other government expenditure / welfare benefits
The government will need to consider how the change in funding method will be communicated to the public
And how it will be received

iv.question: Outline the information that the government may need in order to calculate the present value of the specific healthcare fund using the 'forward funding' method.

Indicative Solution: The government will need the current information relating to its population as a starting point, which should include:
To project forward the country's population in future periods, the government will need:
Time horizon of the funding / projection period
Distribution of morbidity by various diseases / treatment / care types
Migration data may be needed to estimate population changes
To estimate the future costs of providing the support, the government will need:
e.g. whether it is direct provision, lump sum cash payment or regular income
Costs of healthcare by treatment types
Calculate a specific healthcare fund such that ear-marked taxation will, over the period, meet the intended State healthcare provision

5.label: Question 5
5.scenario: Company A is an established health and care insurer that specialises in individual and group Private Medical Insurance (PMI) business in Country X. Country X's healthcare facilities have been deteriorating over recent years, and there had also been a pandemic outbreak in the last 18 months. Company A has made significant financial losses over the last 2 years.

i.question: Suggest possible reasons for Company A's significant financial losses.

Pricing / Claims Experience: The products were wrongly priced hence premiums are proving inadequate to meet claims
The company has weak underwriting procedures hence is susceptible to anti-selection
Claims management and assessment procedures are weak hence the company has been suffering from fraud
Claim Amounts: The actual claims costs / amounts were higher than assumed in pricing
Which could be due to changes in healthcare providers
Or medical inflation was higher than expected
Margins allowed for in pricing assumptions may not be adequate
Excesses may not have been adequately indexed
So that there were far more smaller claims than expected
And /or claims amounts were higher than expected
This may have been due to medical advances which have meant that cost of new procedures and treatments is more than priced
Claim Frequency: The actual claims incidences were higher than assumed in pricing
Which could be due to misestimation of medical procedures
Additional procedure caused by pandemic related causes
And the policy terms and conditions did not explicitly exclude pandemic related claim.
The pandemic may have also resulted in more hospitalizations hence frequency of Claims has increased significantly
Additional claims may have arisen due to the pandemic, even though they ultimately were not pandemic-related

e.g. individuals who thought they were suffering from long-term effects of the pandemic (e.g. long covid) may have sought a diagnosis, but were then diagnosed of a different fatigue-related cause that was treated through their PMI policy
Deteriorating health facilities have meant that only a few medical providers can meet client expectations and the suppliers have increased prices due to high demand
Poor health facilities may have also led to a generally worsening morbidity experience in the company

The healthcare providers / hospital may be experiencing higher operational costs (e.g. upgrading the medical facilities), and the operational costs are being passed on in the form of higher treatment costs
Expenses: Expenses have been more than expected
This might have been due to underestimation of expense assumption in pricing
Other causes of higher expenses may have been due to the need to recruit specialist medical staff which could be expensive
There could have been other one-off expenses. E.g. legal fees to deal with complaints fines / penalties imposed by the regulators
Higher expenses may be due to poor expense controls
This might be due to changes in working practices as a result of the pandemic
Expense inflation may have also led to higher than expected expenses
The healthcare providers / hospital may be being refurbished and the refurbishment costs being passed on in the form of higher treatment costs
Commission paid may have been higher than expected
Renewal rates / New business volume: Higher rate of non-renewals has meant that the company has fewer policies to meet fixed overheads hence higher expense per policy
Customers may be unhappy with the deteriorating conditions hence the higher non-renewals
There may be more non-renewals if the PMI policyholders did not realise that their policy terms do not actually cover treatments directly relating to the pandemic
Which could lead to individuals (and companies) questioning the worth of their PMI policies
And such non-renewals are likely to be selective (leaving those in worse health), which would lead to worse claims experience
Volume of new business may have been lower than expected, which could be due to:
Large corporate clients went out of business
General economic downturn and less people can afford PMI
Damage to brand image / reputation because of policyholder's complaints
Reducing business volume coupled with rising expenses would serve to exacerbate the financial loss
Mix of business: More smaller sized policies were sold, which make lower contributions to overheads

More policyholders may have been switching into products with a lower profit margin, e.g. individuals may only be able to afford the budget (rather than comprehensive) PMI products in the current climate
Lower premiums from the group PMI products may be a result of companies having fewer employees due to e.g. pandemic-related redundancies / furloughed employees
Any change in the mix of business could lead to losses if there were cross-subsidies in the pricing basis
Reserving / Capital Requirements: There has been a significant increase in reserving and capital requirements
This may be due to change in regulatory requirements
Investment: The assets held by the company may have performed badly over that period
This could be due to a downturn in economic conditions
Or bad investment decision / policy taken by Company A
Company A may have had to realise assets in unfavourable conditions
e.g. due to a large number of pandemic-related claims
Other reasons: The company may be lacking an appropriate reinsurance arrangement to ensure a sustainable claims experience
There may have been a default by:
Other third party service providers (e.g. policy administration / claims management)
Intermediaries who are holding a significant amount of premiums
The products may have un-clear and ambiguous definitions / product features such that the company has ended up paying more claims than initially intended.
The regulator may have imposed a significant fine on the company

ii.question: Describe the process that Company A may take for investigating claims experience.

Stage 1 – Collect / validate / cleanse d: The data should be sufficient to be credible
But not too old to be irrelevant
An investigation period covering the past 3 to 5 years is generally acceptable
Types of data required include policy data, claims data, renewal data etc
Data checks should be carried out for validation and reasonability
And remove any incorrect or irrelevant data.
Validate data against external sources, e.g. reinsurer / market / population data
Stage 2 – Split data into reasonably hom: Next stage is to subdivide data into subsets that are relevant

For example: type of contract, age, gender, duration in force, smoker/ non-smoker status, industry (especially for group rates), occupation, location, amount of excess, NCD level, hospital band / group
The subsets should be homogenous but still be credible
Morbidity rates would be split into:
Incident year (so trends can be identified / investigated)
Stage 3 – Perform Actual vs Expected cal: The morbidity rates can be determined using an exposed to risk approach
Policies data is used to calculate the time for which each live was exposed to the risk of sickness
The principle of correspondence is important to apply
Lives are only included in a particular age cohort if they had fallen sick on that particular date
They would have been included as a claim if they had received the medical treatments
This is based on the age definition. E.g. age last birthday or age next birthday
For data relating to recent periods, the company would need to make appropriate allowance for incurred but not reported claims in its claims data
Compare actual morbidity rates and claims costs to those that were expected. i.e. an A vs E analysis
Adjustments may need to be made if there have been changes over the period of the investigation

e.g. in respect of target market, underwriting and claims management, policy terms and conditions, the economic environment, state benefits, attitudes to claiming, costs of medical procedures, changes to healthcare providers, medical advances
